content.text: Domain adaptation is continued pre-training of a general-purpose language model on a large corpus of unlabelled domain-specific text. The goal is not to teach the model a new task — it is to shift its internal representations, statistical priors, and vocabulary competence toward a target domain before any task-specific fine-tuning begins. A model adapted to biomedical literature understands the statistical relationships between terms like "myocardial infarction", "troponin", and "STEMI" the same way it understands everyday words — fluently, without needing those terms to appear repeatedly in each training example.

The canonical study is "Don't Stop Pretraining" (Gururangan et al., 2020), which systematically showed that domain-adaptive pre-training (DAPT) consistently improves downstream task performance across four domains (biomedical, computer science, news, reviews) and eight tasks — even when the domain corpus is only a few gigabytes and training runs for a fraction of the original pre-training budget.

General pre-training corpora (Common Crawl, Wikipedia, books) are dominated by everyday language. Domain-specific terminology appears rarely, and when it does, the surrounding context is often general rather than technical. Three consequences follow:
: the model assigns low probability to domain terms at every prediction step. High perplexity on domain text indicates the model is constantly "surprised" by the vocabulary it encounters.

: compound medical terms like "tachyarrhythmia" or "nephrotoxicity" get split into many subword tokens because the vocabulary was optimised for general English. Each extra token wastes context window space and forces the model to reconstruct meaning from fragments.

: the embedding vectors for domain terms were updated only by the rare occurrences in general text. They do not capture the nuanced relationships between concepts that a domain expert would recognise.

attrs.alt: Top: three-stage pipeline — general pre-training on web text → domain adaptation on unlabelled domain corpus → task fine-tuning on labelled data → domain-expert model. Middle: token distribution shift visualised — general corpus has medical terms rarely (faded), domain corpus has them prominently (bold blue), after adaptation the model assigns correct probabilities. Bottom: table of domain-adapted models by field (biomedical, legal, finance, code, scientific) with training corpora and key advantages.

content.text: Successful domain adaptation typically follows a three-stage pattern:

: train on a large diverse corpus (trillions of tokens). This gives the model language understanding, world knowledge, and reasoning capability. You rarely do this yourself — you start from a publicly available checkpoint.

: continue training on domain-specific unlabelled text (millions to billions of tokens). Use the same language modelling objective (causal LM or masked LM) with a lower learning rate to avoid catastrophic forgetting of general knowledge.
: fine-tune the domain-adapted model on task-specific labelled data. This stage benefits from the richer domain representations from Stage 2.
Domain adaptation is structurally identical to pre-training but starts from a checkpoint instead of random weights. The key differences are the learning rate schedule and the data:

from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, Trainer, TrainingArguments
from datasets import load_dataset

model_name = "meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf"
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_name)
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)

# Domain corpus: PubMed abstracts (~100M tokens for a small run)
dataset = load_dataset("json", data_files="pubmed_abstracts.jsonl")

def tokenize(batch):
    return tokenizer(
        batch["text"],
        truncation=True,
        max_length=2048,
        return_overflowing_tokens=True,  # pack multiple documents
        stride=0,
    )

tokenized = dataset.map(tokenize, batched=True, remove_columns=["text"])

args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir="./llama2-bio",
    num_train_epochs=1,             # domain adaptation typically runs 1-3 epochs
    per_device_train_batch_size=4,
    gradient_accumulation_steps=16, # effective batch 64
    learning_rate=2e-5,             # much lower than original pre-training (~3e-4)
    lr_scheduler_type="cosine",
    warmup_ratio=0.01,
    bf16=True,
    save_steps=500,
    logging_steps=50,
    # No evaluation set needed for domain adaptation (unsupervised objective)
)

trainer = Trainer(model=model, args=args, train_dataset=tokenized["train"])
trainer.train()
Lower than original pre-training to preserve general knowledge
More risks catastrophic forgetting; diminishing returns early
DAPT paper found gains start at ~1B; plateau varies by domain
Long context captures document-level domain structure
Short warm-up — model already converged from Stage 1

Full continued pre-training requires the same GPU memory as training the base model — expensive for large models. PEFT methods can be applied to domain adaptation too, keeping base weights frozen and training only low-rank adapters or prefix vectors on the domain corpus:

from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model, TaskType
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf")

# LoRA for domain adaptation: use higher rank than task fine-tuning
# because domain adaptation requires more representational shift
config = LoraConfig(
    r=64,            # higher rank for domain adaptation (vs r=16 for task FT)
    lora_alpha=128,
    target_modules=["q_proj", "v_proj", "k_proj", "o_proj",
                    "gate_proj", "up_proj", "down_proj"],  # include FFN for domain shift
    lora_dropout=0.05,
    bias="none",
    task_type=TaskType.CAUSAL_LM,
)

model = get_peft_model(model, config)
model.print_trainable_parameters()
# trainable params: 159,383,552 || all params: 6,738,415,616 || trainable%: 2.37

# Training is identical to full continued pre-training
# but uses ~4x less memory (no optimizer states for frozen params)

The trade-off: PEFT domain adaptation is memory-efficient but slightly less effective than full continued pre-training, because the frozen base weights cannot shift their representations — only the low-rank perturbation adjusts. For very large models (70B+) where full pre-training is impractical, PEFT domain adaptation is often the only feasible option.
The quality of the domain corpus matters more than quantity. Common quality issues and mitigations:
Train a classifier to score domain relevance
Mix abstracts with full-text where available

Some domains benefit from extending the tokeniser vocabulary with domain-specific tokens. Medical models like BioMedLM and BioBERT train new BPE vocabularies on domain text so that common medical compound words become single tokens rather than many subword pieces:

from tokenizers import ByteLevelBPETokenizer
from transformers import AutoTokenizer

# Train a new BPE vocabulary on domain text
tokenizer = ByteLevelBPETokenizer()
tokenizer.train(
    files=["pubmed_corpus.txt"],
    vocab_size=32000,
    min_frequency=2,
    special_tokens=["<|endoftext|>", "<pad>"],
)
tokenizer.save_model("bio_tokenizer")

# Compare tokenisation of a medical term:
general_tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
bio_tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bio_tokenizer")

term = "tachyarrhythmia"
print(general_tokenizer.tokenize(term))
# ['t', 'ach', 'y', 'arr', 'hyth', 'mia']  → 6 tokens

print(bio_tokenizer.tokenize(term))
# ['tachyarrhythmia']  → 1 token (seen enough times in domain corpus)

Vocabulary extension requires re-initialising the embedding table rows for new tokens (randomly or from similar existing tokens), then training these new embeddings during domain adaptation. The trade-off is that the extended vocabulary requires extending the model's embedding matrix, increasing its memory footprint slightly.

The main risk in domain adaptation is forgetting general capabilities acquired during pre-training. A model that becomes too specialised on biomedical text may lose its ability to reason about general topics, follow instructions, or write coherent prose. Three strategies mitigate this:
: mix domain corpus with a small fraction (5–20%) of general corpus. The model continues to see general language and retains its broad capabilities.
: using 1e-5 to 5e-5 (vs 1e-4 to 3e-4 for task fine-tuning) limits how far weights move from their pre-trained values per step.
: adds a regularisation term that penalises changes to parameters deemed important for the original task. Computationally expensive but effective at preserving general knowledge.

In practice, data mixing is the most widely used approach. Code LLaMA, for example, was adapted from LLaMA 2 using a 500B token corpus that mixed code with 8% general English — enough to preserve natural-language instruction following.
Two complementary metrics track adaptation quality:

: measures how well the model predicts domain text. Should decrease steadily during adaptation. A well-adapted biomedical model typically achieves perplexity of 5–15 on PubMed text, vs 30–50 for a general model.

: after Stage 3 fine-tuning, evaluate on domain-specific benchmarks: BLURB for biomedical NLP, LexGLUE for legal, FiNER for finance, HumanEval for code. This is the definitive measure of whether domain adaptation helped.

import math
import torch
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
from datasets import load_dataset

def domain_perplexity(model_name: str, test_texts: list[str]) -> float:
    """Compute perplexity on domain text as an adaptation quality metric."""
    model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_name)
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
    model.eval()

    total_loss, total_tokens = 0.0, 0
    for text in test_texts:
        inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt", max_length=512, truncation=True)
        with torch.no_grad():
            out = model(**inputs, labels=inputs["input_ids"])
        # out.loss is mean cross-entropy over tokens in this sequence
        n_tokens = inputs["input_ids"].numel() - 1  # predict all but first
        total_loss += out.loss.item() * n_tokens
        total_tokens += n_tokens

    return math.exp(total_loss / total_tokens)

# Typical values after adaptation:
# General LLaMA-2 on PubMed: ~42 perplexity
# After DAPT on PubMed:      ~11 perplexity  (4× improvement)
pubmed_test = load_dataset("pubmed", split="test")["abstract"][:500]
print(f"Adapted model PPL: {domain_perplexity('llama2-bio', pubmed_test):.1f}")
Domain adaptation and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) both address the gap between a general model and a specific knowledge domain, but they do so differently:

In practice, the two are complementary: domain adaptation improves the model's base competence in the domain (vocabulary, reasoning patterns), while RAG provides access to specific up-to-date facts and documents at query time. Production medical AI systems often use both.
Production systems often serve multiple domains simultaneously. Two strategies scale domain adaptation to this setting:
: adapt once per domain in sequence, using EWC (Elastic Weight Consolidation) or replay buffers to prevent forgetting prior domains. Simple but each new domain risks overwriting the previous.
: sample from all domain corpora simultaneously with domain-proportional mixing. More stable — gradient steps see all domains every update — but requires all domain data upfront.

For PEFT, multi-domain adaptation is cleaner: train a separate set of LoRA weights (or adapters) per domain, keep the base frozen, and swap modules at inference time. Memory cost is O(n × adapter_size) and domains never interfere:

from peft import PeftModel

base_model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf")

def load_domain_model(domain: str):
    """Load base + domain-specific LoRA weights at inference time."""
    return PeftModel.from_pretrained(
        base_model,
        f"adapters/{domain}",   # each domain has its own adapter directory
        is_trainable=False,
    )

# At serving time: keep base in GPU memory, swap only the adapters (~8 MB each)
medical_model  = load_domain_model("biomedical")
legal_model    = load_domain_model("legal")
finance_model  = load_domain_model("finance")
is the standard in production: a single base model serves all domains and the adapter swap latency is negligible (< 1 ms on GPU).
Domain adaptation failures are usually diagnosable from a small set of symptoms. The table below maps each symptom to its cause and fix:
Task accuracy drops after domain adaptation
Mix 5–20% general corpus; lower learning rate
Domain corpus too small or too clean
Scale data; include diverse document types
Learning rate too high for the pre-trained weights
Dataset contaminated with general web text
Audit corpus; apply stricter domain filters
Optimizer state (Adam) triples memory of weights
Switch to LoRA; use bf16 + gradient checkpointing
High domain perplexity but poor entity handling
Domain OOV terms fragmented by tokenizer
Extend vocabulary with domain BPE tokens

A quick diagnostic checklist before filing a bug: (1) evaluate perplexity on held-out domain text before and after, (2) evaluate a general benchmark (e.g. HellaSwag) to confirm no catastrophic forgetting, (3) inspect sample generations for domain terminology. Perplexity drop ≥ 20% on domain text with ≤ 5% regression on general benchmarks is the target signal.
Domain adaptation is continued pre-training on unlabelled domain text, shifting the model's priors and vocabulary to a target domain before task fine-tuning.
The DAPT paper showed consistent gains across four domains and eight tasks even with modest compute budgets.
Use lower LR (1e-5 to 5e-5), 1–3 epochs, and optionally mix 5–20% general corpus to prevent catastrophic forgetting.
PEFT (LoRA with higher rank r=32–64) enables domain adaptation on large models without full continued pre-training memory costs.
Track domain perplexity (should drop 2–5×) and downstream task accuracy as complementary evaluation signals.
Domain adaptation and RAG are complementary: adaptation improves vocabulary and reasoning; RAG supplies specific up-to-date facts.
